coredata.dc:title: Thin film nanocomposite polyamide membrane doped with amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots for organic solvent nanofiltration
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shuxuan
Liu, Shaoxiao
Su, Baowei
Gao, Xueli
Gao, Congjie
coredata.dc:description: Thin film nanocomposite (TFN) organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN) membrane with improved separation performance was fabricated via interfacial polymerization reaction between amine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (af-GQDs) doped aqueous monomer solution of m-phenylenediamine (MPD) and n-hexane solution containing trimesoyl chloride (TMC) monomer. We emphasize the employment of extra-low content for both af-GQDs and MPD, so that we could maximize the function of these nanomaterials and eliminate their aggregation at the same time. The af-GQDs nanomaterials could regulate the interfacial polymerization process and could be strongly anchored in the polyamide film via covalent bonding, hence could prevent their loss while providing more channels for faster solvent penetration. As a result, the optimized OSN membrane, TFN-50, shows a super-smooth surface, with a surface roughness as low as 4.3 ± 0.2 nm, and excellent pure solvent permeances which reach 135.8, 112.7, and 69.8 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1 for methanol, DMF and ethanol, respectively, as well as a very high Rhodamine B (RDB, 479 Da) rejection of up to 99.2%, and an ethanol permeance of 61.4 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1. Furthermore, the TFN-50 OSN membrane still shows excellent filtration performance after being submerged in DMF for 336 h or continuous crossflow filtration with Rose bengal (RB, 1017 Da)/DMF solution for 136 h, demonstrating its excellent solvent resistance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Interfacial polymerization
Amine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (af-GQDs)
Thin film nanocomposite (TFN)
Organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of TFN OSN membrane incorporated with af-GQDs
Impact of the af-GQDs content on OSN membrane performance
Performance comparison with other OSN membranes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of the fabrication process of the OSN membrane.
Reaction equations during the OSN membrane fabrication [26]. (a) MPD with TMC, (b) af-GQDs with TMC, (c) af-GQDs with TMC and MPD.
Impact of the af-GQDs content on the OSN membrane performance (MPD content: 0.05 wt%).

FTIR spectra of the PI substrate, TFC membrane (uncrosslinked, MPD content: 0.05 wt%, af-GQDs content: 0 mg L−1), baseline TFC membrane (MPD content: 0.05 wt%, af-GQDs content: 0 mg L−1), TFN-50 membrane (MPD content: 0.05 wt%, af-GQDs content: 50 mg L−1).
The SEM images of OSN membranes: (a) baseline TFC, (b) TFN-25, (c) TFN-50, (d) TFN-75, (e) TFN-100, (f) TFN-200, (g) (PA/PI)XA.
The AFM images of OSN membranes: (a) baseline TFC, (b) TFN-25, (c) TFN-50, (d) TFN-75, (e) TFN-100, (f) TFN-200, (g) (PA/PI)XA.
AFM image and corresponding height profiles of the selective layer in the baseline TFC (a) and TFN-50 (b) membrane.
The rejections of TFN-50 OSN membrane for dyes (a) and PEGs (b).
The pure solvent permeance of the TFN-50 OSN membrane.

The correlation of pure solvent permeance and solvent properties (δ
p,s
MV
s
−1 η
s
−1) for the TFN-50 OSN membrane.
The durability test of the TFN-50 OSN membrane in DMF (a) and NMP (b) at 80 °C.
The continuous operation filtration performance of TFN-50 OSN membrane.
Mean pore size, standard deviation, surface pore density and surface porosity of the baseline and the TFN OSN membranes.
Filtration performance of the TFN-50 OSN membrane and some OSN membranes in literature works.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Notes: PVDF: polyvinylidene fluoride; t-Cu-TCPP: a kind of metal−organic frameworks nanosheet; PA: polyamide; PI: polyimide; mZIF-8: a kind of metal−organic frameworks nanoparticle; HPAN: hydrolyzed polyacrylonitrile; TpTD: a kind of porous covalent organic frameworks; PEI: polyetherimide; CaAlg: a kind of hydrogel; HKUST: a kind of metal−organic frameworks nanoparticle; HPEI: hyperbranched polyethylenimine; PP: polypropylene; PBI: polybenzimidazole; BADGE: Bisphenol A diglycidyl ether; PASS: polyarylene sulfide sulfone; ATA: amide tertiary amine; HPAN: hydrolyzed polyacrylonitrile; QPVD: quaternized P(VC-r-DMAEMA).

head.ce:title: Thin film nanocomposite polyamide membrane doped with amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots for organic solvent nanofiltration

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Theory and Technology, Ministry of Education, Ocean University of China, 238 Songling Road, Qingdao, 266100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Theory and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Theory and Technology, Ministry of Education, Ocean University of China, 238 Songling Road, Qingdao, 266100, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Ocean University of China, 238 Songling Road, Qingdao, 266100, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Ocean University of China, 238 Songling Road, Qingdao, 266100, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Ocean University of China, 238 Songling Road, Qingdao, 266100, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Ocean University of China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Key Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Theory and Technology (Ocean University of China), Ministry of Education, 238 Songling Road, Qingdao, 266100, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Theory and Technology (Ocean University of China), Ministry of Education

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: First authors, Shuxuan Li and Shaoxiao Liu contribute equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thin film nanocomposite (TFN) organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN) membrane with improved separation performance was fabricated via interfacial polymerization reaction between amine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (af-GQDs) doped aqueous monomer solution of m-phenylenediamine (MPD) and n-hexane solution containing trimesoyl chloride (TMC) monomer. We emphasize the employment of extra-low content for both af-GQDs and MPD, so that we could maximize the function of these nanomaterials and eliminate their aggregation at the same time. The af-GQDs nanomaterials could regulate the interfacial polymerization process and could be strongly anchored in the polyamide film via covalent bonding, hence could prevent their loss while providing more channels for faster solvent penetration. As a result, the optimized OSN membrane, TFN-50, shows a super-smooth surface, with a surface roughness as low as 4.3 ± 0.2 nm, and excellent pure solvent permeances which reach 135.8, 112.7, and 69.8 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1 for methanol, DMF and ethanol, respectively, as well as a very high Rhodamine B (RDB, 479 Da) rejection of up to 99.2%, and an ethanol permeance of 61.4 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1. Furthermore, the TFN-50 OSN membrane still shows excellent filtration performance after being submerged in DMF for 336 h or continuous crossflow filtration with Rose bengal (RB, 1017 Da)/DMF solution for 136 h, demonstrating its excellent solvent resistance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN) technology has vast potential for catalyst recovery, solvent recycling, and molecule fractionation due to its better energy efficiency than traditional technologies [1]. As the crucial point of OSN technology, OSN membranes possess a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) between 200 and 1000 Da (Da), and a pore size between 0.5 and 2.0 nm [2]. Therefore, they could retain small organic solutes such as catalysts, dye molecules, and so on, while permitting organic solvents such as isopropyl alcohol and so on, to permeate through.

At present, OSN membranes are primarily categorized into integrally skinned asymmetric OSN membranes and thin film composite (TFC) OSN membranes [3]. The former is frequently prepared via phase separation method, while the latter is usually fabricated via interfacial polymerization method, coating method, or layer-by-layer method. Interfacial polymerization method has become a research hotspot as its process could be easily regulated. The OSN membrane made via interfacial polymerization method features a selectivity layer on a porous support. This selective layer plays a dominant role for the filtration performance of OSN membranes and it is generally prepared via the reaction between acryl chloride monomer and amine monomer. The typical reaction monomers are m-phenylenediamine (MPD) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC). In recent years, there are also some other new monomers [4–6]. In addition, researchers found that the overall performance of OSN membrane could be further improved by the incorporation of nanomaterials such as graphene oxide (GO) [7], covalent organic frameworks (COFs) [8,9], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [10], SiO2 [11], TiO2 [12], and metal-organic frameworks [13].

Among these nanomaterials, GO displays broad prospects in membrane preparation due to its excellent thermal, chemical stability and abundant oxygen-containing functional groups. However, GO possesses a large sheet size (usually larger than 500 nm) [14–16] and tends to distribute parallel to the selectivity layer, which could increase the solvent transport path and thus results in poor separation performance [17]. Comparatively, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with lateral size of smaller than 10 nm and the same functional groups as GO nanosheets seem more suitable for membrane fabrication [17]. In our previous work [18], we doped GQDs into forward osmosis membrane to enhance its separation performance. As a result, GQDs made the selectivity layer thinner and built many mass transfer channels therein. Thus, the GQDs incorporated forward osmosis membrane obtained a higher water flux of 12.9 L m−2 h−1. Prof. Jiang's group [19] enhanced the water permeance of nanofiltration membrane via a GQDs modulation method by approximately 5.8 times higher than that of the GQDs-free nanofiltration membrane. Meanwhile, the GQDs also offer better anti-fouling performance to the resultant nanofiltration membrane, as the GQDs make the resultant nanofiltration membrane more hydrophilic and smoother.

Although GO or GQDs could enhance the separation performance and other performance, such as anti-fouling and chlorine resistance, the membranes incorporated with GO or GQDs tend to exhibit poor stability performance since GO or GQDs nanomaterials are lack of enough functional groups to crosslink with polymer [20]. To overcome this problem, researchers tried to functionalize these nanomaterials, and thus endowed the nanomaterials-doped membranes with better performance [2,21]. For instance, Prof. Chen [22] successfully fabricated GO nanosheets with acyl chloride groups and doped them into the polyamide layer, thus successfully boosted both water flux and salt rejection of the resultant nanofiltration membrane. In our previous case [23], we have succeeded in manufacturing an OSN membrane doped with amine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (af-GQDs) nanomaterials. We employed the af-GQDs as aqueous phase additive and significantly improved the filtration performance of the obtained OSN membrane while maintain its excellent durability performance. However, the optimized OSN membrane exhibited a much rough surface with a roughness of 35.9 ± 2.7 nm and a low ethanol permeance of only 32 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1, since we applied a 2.0 wt% aqueous phase MPD solution and 0.3 wt% 1,2,4,5-benzene tetracarboxylic acyl chloride/n-hexane solution, both of which are much higher.

Our recent works [24,25] proved that extra-low content of MPD (0.05 wt%) could help to construct an extra-smooth and extra-thin polyamide layer with improved solvent permeance. The extra-smooth surface could effectively reduce the adsorption and trap of substances on the membrane surface, which is favorable for a stable solvent permeance in the long-time filtration. Meanwhile, if there are some species trapped in membrane surface, they would be more easily washed away [23]. These give us inspiration that we could combine the advantages of extra-low MPD content and the addition of af-GQDs, and thus successfully obtained an af-GQDs-doped OSN membrane with excellent filtration performance via a simple fabrication process. Hence, in this work, we firstly constructed a baseline TFC OSN membrane with a smooth surface by using extra-low MPD content, and then introduced the af-GQDs into the aqueous phase MPD solution to boost its filtration performance. By using ultra-low content for af-GQDs, we aim to maximize the function of these nanomaterials and eliminate their aggregation at the same time, and used them to manipulate the interfacial polymerization process, and to provide more channels and thinner selectivity layer for faster solvent penetration. Via the cooperative interaction between extra-low MPD content and the extra-low af-GQDs content, we aim to prepare an OSN membrane with an extra-smooth surface and high separation performance using a simplified procedure process.
The materials used in this work were shown in Table 1 .

The fabrication procedure of the af-GQDs with a mean size of 7.2 nm and a mean thickness of 1.6 nm could be seen in our previous work [23]. The detailed production of the PI substrate was stated in our previous work [8] and the preparation process of the selective layer was shown in Fig. 1 . Firstly, the PI substrate membrane taken out from the ultrapure water was fixed on a glass plate, and the residual water drops were removed by a squeeze roller. Shortly after that, aqueous phase monomer solution containing MPD (0.025 wt% ∼ 1.0 wt%) and af-GQDs (0∼ 200 mg L−1) nanosheets was poured on the PI substrate membrane surface and was kept for 8 s before it was poured off. Subsequently, the remaining water drops were removed by the squeeze roller, and then the PI substrate membrane was desiccated in atmosphere for about 1 min to remove the remaining water drops completely. Afterward, the PI substrate was submerged in 0.15 wt% TMC/n-hexane solution for 6 s to conduct the interfacial polymerization reaction to form the skin layer. Afterward, the virgin membrane was crosslinked with 10.0 wt% HDA/IPA solution at 60 °C for 30 min and was then activated in pure DMF liquid at 80 °C for a period of time (0 ∼ 60 min). In this way, the OSN membrane incorporated with af-GQDs was obtained and stored in ethanol for further filtration performance test. This membrane was termed as “TFN-X″, where “X” represents af-GQDs content. For comparison, baseline OSN membrane was also prepared using the same procedure but without the addition of af-GQDs, and was termed as baseline TFC, as well as the (PA/PI)XA membrane was prepared according to our previous work [8]. For detecting the thickness of the selectivity layer in the baseline TFC and TFN-50 membranes, the free-standing films were prepared under the same interfacial polymerization parameters of the baseline TFC and the TFN-50 membranes, respectively.

The reaction equations existed in the membrane fabrication process were depicted in Fig. 2 . MPD could react with TMC to construct a polyamide layer (Fig. 2 (a)). As af-GQDs nanomaterials possess –NH2 groups, they could also react with TMC, and thus af-GQDs could be strongly bonded with the polyamide film via covalent bonds (as shown in Fig. 2 (b,c)).

Surface topography was obtained by scanning electron microscope (SEM) and Atomic force microscope (AFM). In addition, the thickness of the selectivity layer was obtained by AFM according to literature works [6,27]. The chemical composition of the prepared OSN membrane was detected by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR). The details of the membrane characterization, containing SEM, AFM, and FTIR, were described in the Supplementary materials. The test methods for pore size and pore size distribution were described in detail in our recent work [28].

All the separation performance test was carried out on a lab-scale crossflow filtration platform. Prior to the test, the OSN membranes were pre-conditioned for 30 min at 1.2 MPa. During the test, the temperature of the feed solution and the operating pressure was fixed at 25 ± 1 °C and 1.0 MPa, respectively.

We measured the filtration performance of the OSN membranes using 100 mg L−1 RDB/ethanol solution and the pure solvent permeance using different solvents (as listed in Table S1). The solvent permeance (P, L m−2 h−1 MPa−1) was acquired using Eq. (1).
(1)
P =
Δ m
ρ × Δ t × A × Δ p
in which
Δ m
(g) represents the mass change of the permeate during
Δ t
(h),
ρ
(g L−1) represents the solvent density,
A
(m2) represents the filtration area, and
Δ p
(MPa) represents the operating pressure.

The solute rejection (R, %) was calculated by Eq. (2).
(2)
R =
C F
−
C P
C F
× 100 %
where
C F
and
C P
(mg L−1) represent the solute concentration of the feed and permeate, respectively.

For measuring the MWCO of the OSN membranes, we used aqueous solutions including 100 mg L−1 PEGs or ethanol solution containing 100 mg L−1 dyes with different molecular weight as feed. The temperature of the feed and the operating pressure was fixed at 25 ± 1 °C and 0.5 MPa, respectively. The PEGs or dye rejection was calculated by Eq. (2).

In order to assess the durability performance of the OSN membrane, we employed the static immersion method and continuous operation method. The detailed procedure was described in the Supplementary materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of TFN OSN membrane incorporated with af-GQDs
Impact of the af-GQDs content on OSN membrane performance
ce:section.ce:para: We explored the impacts of both activation time and MPD content on the OSN membrane performance and the results is listed in the Supplementary materials. As a result, we fixed the activation time and MPD content at 30 min and 0.05 wt%, respectively, for the subsequent membrane fabrication. To further enhance the OSN membrane performance, we explored the impact of af-GQDs content on the TFN OSN membrane performance. As depicted in Fig. 3 , the af-GQDs content has almost no effect on the RDB rejection, but shows an obvious influence on the ethanol permeance.

The ethanol permeance firstly raises and then falls as the increment of the af-GQDs content, reaching the highest value of 61.4 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1 at af-GQDs concentration of 50 mg L−1. There are several points for explaining the increased ethanol permeance. With the addition of the af-GQDs nanomaterials, many channels could be provided between af-GQDs and the polyamide polymer, which is beneficial for fast solvent penetration [29,30]. Meanwhile, af-GQDs possess a larger lamellar structure (7.2 nm) and a great number of –NH2 groups, which could impede the diffusion rate of MPD molecules towards the reaction zone via the steric hindrance and hydrogen bonding, and hence the incorporation of af-GQDs is conducive to forming a thinner polyamide layer. In detail, as depicted in Fig. 7, the thickness of selectivity layer in the baseline TFC and the TFN-50 membrane is about 44 nm and 8.6 nm, respectively. A similar phenomenon also appeared in our previous work [23]. The thinner selectivity layer always shows lower solvent penetration resistance, and thus is conducive to increasing the ethanol permeance. In addition, the impeding effect of af-GQDs on MPD molecular diffusion also could bring a looser structure to the selectivity layer [31], and thus the TFN-50 membrane possesses the higher pore surface pore density and surface porosity (as shown in Fig. S1 and Table 2 ), which is favorable for solvent penetration [28]. Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 2, the prepared af-GQDs could react with TMC to embed the af-GQDs in the polyamide layer, and thus the resultant polymer possesses a larger free volume, which could also provide more solvent permeation channels [32–34].

When the af-GQDs content is more than 50 mg L−1, the ethanol permeance begins to drop, which is caused by af-GQDs aggregation appearing at high content [23]. In this case, the aggregated af-GQDs would have less impeding effect on the diffusion of MPD molecules. Hence, more MPD molecules would participate in the interfacial polymerization reaction, which results in a denser polyamide layer [35], as well as lower surface pore density and surface porosity (as depicted in Table 2). Meanwhile, the aggregation of af-GQDs could reduce the amount of the solvent penetration channels [36], which could increase the solvent penetration resistance. Therefore, the ethanol permeance decreased significantly.

The FTIR spectra of the OSN membranes are depicted in Fig. 4 , it could be observed that all the membranes possess peaks at 1365, 1722, and 1779 cm−1, corresponding to the C–N–C stretching peak, symmetric stretching of carbonyl C=O, and asymmetric stretching peak of PI, respectively [37,38]. The peaks at 1650 and 1536 cm−1 are resulted from the amide groups in the baseline TFC, uncrosslinked TFC and TFN-50 OSN membranes, which proves that polyamide layer is constructed [39]. Besides, the baseline TFC membrane possesses a weakened characteristic peak at 1100 cm−1, which proves that the crosslinking agent HDA successfully reacts with the polyimide and converts its imide groups to amide groups.

Figs. 5 and 6
display the SEM and AFM topographies of the (PA/PI)XA, the baseline TFC, and the TFN OSN membranes prepared under different af-GQDs content. In detail, the baseline TFC (Fig. 5 (a)) and the TFN (Fig. 5 (b ∼ f)) OSN membranes prepared by using extra-low MPD content present a completely different surface morphology from (PA/PI)XA (Fig. 5 (g)) membrane prepared by using high MPD content in our previous work [8], where the surface of the TFC and the TFN OSN membrane is smoother and more regular. This is due to the fact that lower MPD content used in the interfacial polymerization process could minimize the formation of nanobubbles [40].

As depicted in Fig. 6, a similar surface also appears in the AFM images, the surface roughness (Ra) of the baseline TFC OSN membrane is only about 5.5 ± 0.2 nm, which is much lower than that of the (PA/PI)XA OSN membrane (Ra = 26.1 ± 1.4 nm, as shown in Fig. 6 (g)) fabricated in our previous work [8]. The reason for this phenomenon is that the lower reaction monomer content we adopted for the baseline TFC OSN membrane could reduce the polymerization rate, and thus results in a smoother surface [41]. As the raise of the af-GQDs content, the surface roughness firstly declines and then raises, and the TFN-50 OSN membrane possesses the lowest surface roughness (Ra = 4.3 ± 0.2 nm). The declined surface roughness could be due to the impeded MPD molecule diffusion [42], as stated in Section 3.1.1 . A similar phenomenon also appears in the work of Prof. Xu [43], in which the authors successfully reduce the surface roughness by the addition of multi-walled carbon nanotubes. However, the surface roughness of the TFN OSN membrane made under 75, 100 and 200 mg L−1 af-GQDs content gradually increased to 4.7 ± 0.3 nm, 8.6 ± 0.5 nm, and 11.7 ± 0.4 nm, respectively. The increased surface roughness is caused by the fact that af-GQDs would agglomerate when their content is too high, thus form af-GQDs aggregation with larger size and result in bulge-like structure on the membrane surface [44].

As described in Fig. 8 (a), the TFN-50 OSN membrane exhibits excellent rejections for RDB, EY, CBB and RB, all of which are much higher than 99.0%. The rejection of ST is 86.5%, since ST has a molecular weight of only 350 Da. As depicted in Fig. 8 (b), the PEGs rejections increases with their molecular weight, where rejections for PEG-200, PEG-400, PEG-600, PEG-800 and PEG-1000 are 73.6%, 92.1%, 96.8%, 97.5%, and 98.7%, respectively. According to the results in Fig. 8, the TFN-50 membrane exhibits an MWCO of approximately 380 Da. Based on th PEGs rejection, the average effective pore diameter of the TFN-50 membrane is about 0.43 nm, as seen in Fig. S2 of the Supplementary materials.

As shown in Fig. 9 , the pure solvent permeances of methanol, DMF, ethanol, DMAc, iso-propanol, n-hexane, and iso-butanol reach 135.8, 112.7, 69.8, 52.3, 13.4, 10.6, 6.0 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1, respectively. Fig. 10 suggests that the pure solvent permeance is nearly proportional to the δ
p,s
MV
s
−1 η
s
−1 value of the solvent, which demonstrates that the TFN-50 OSN membrane shows higher pure solvent permeance for the solvent with lower molecular weight, lower dynamic viscosity, and strong polarity [45,46].

The stability performance of the TFN-50 OSN membrane could be seen in Fig. 11 . As seen in Fig. 11 (a), after the soak in DMF at 80 °C for 336 h, the TFN-50 OSN membrane obtains higher ethanol permeance, however, its RDB rejection is almost unchanged. The raised ethanol permeance is mainly resulted from the fact that the high-temperature soaking in DMF acts as a further solvent activation treatment, which could further dissolve some small molecule polymer chain segments, thus resulting in the boosted ethanol permeance [47,48]. As depicted in Fig. 11 (b), a similar phenomenon also appears during the high-temperature soaking in NMP at 80 °C. These results indicate that the TFN-50 OSN membrane exhibits excellent durability performance.

The result of continuous operation is described in Fig. 12 , the DMF permeance firstly increases, and then decreases gradually to approximately 64 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1. During the first 30 h filtration, DMF further activates the TFN-50 OSN membrane, thus resulting in an increased DMF permeance [47,48]. After that, the DMF permeance begins to decrease, which could be mainly attributed to membrane fouling. However, the RB rejection maintains consistently above 99.0% during the long-time filtration test, which exhibits that the prepared TFN-50 OSN membrane has good stability performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance comparison with other OSN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Table 3 lists the ethanol permeance, solute rejection, and surface roughness (Ra) of the TFN-50 OSN membrane and some other OSN membranes. It could be seen that the TFN-50 OSN membrane is among the highest rank from the aspect of solute rejection. Meanwhile, it also exhibits moderately high ethanol permeance. Furthermore, the TFN-50 OSN membrane is among the smoothest rank from its surface roughness of only 4.3 ± 0.2 nm, which could effectively reduce the adsorption of fouling substance on the membrane surface, and thus could be favorable for a stable solvent permeance in the long-time filtration [19]. Although the PBI–BADGE OSN membrane fabricated in the literature work [49], possesses the highest ethanol permeance of up to 232.0 L m−2 h−1 MPa−1, it shows a poor long-time filtration performance, with the ethanol permeance of only about 59% of the initial ethanol permeance after a continuous filtration for about 48 h. However, in our work, the ethanol permeance of the TFN-50 membrane is almost 92% of the initial ethanol permeance after a continuous filtration of about 130 h (as shown in Fig. 12), which is attributed to the extra-smooth surface of the TFN-50 membrane. Moreover, rather resistance to strong polar solvents such as DMF and NMP was achieved in this work, even the TFN-50 membrane was immersed in DMF and NMP at very high temperature as high as 80 °C for about 350 h, it still possesses excellent solute rejection, proving the advantages of this work for the potential application in solvent system in chemical and related industries. Therefore, the TFN-50 OSN membrane has vast advantages over other advanced OSN membranes.

Furthermore, we compared several works made by other groups [19,27,57–59] as well as our group [23] utilized GQDs or its derivatives for the TFN membrane fabrication, as shown in Table S2. It could be found that the contents of both the reactive monomer and af-GQDs used in this work are lower than those of other works. This could not only bring a decreased cost in membrane fabrication, but also greatly decrease the surface roughness of the OSN membrane prepared in this work as compared with those in other literature works [19,23,57–59]. The extra-low surface could reduce the adsorption or trap of substances on the membrane surface, which is favorable for a stable solvent permeance during the long-time filtration. At last, the adopted interfacial polymerization time in this work is obviously shorter than other works, which could enhance fabrication efficiency.

In summary, we developed a TFN OSN membrane by adopting extra-low MPD concentration (0.05 wt%) and extra-low af-GQDs content (50 mg L−1) during the interfacial polymerization process. Benefiting from the extra-low MPD concentration and the regulation of af-GQDs, the optimized TFN-50 OSN membrane shows a superb-smooth surface with a roughness of only 4.3 ± 0.2 nm. Additionally, the af-GQDs effectively improve the ethanol permeance by 50% through their steric hindrance and hydrogen bonding with MPD molecules as well as the built solvent penetration channels provided by the af-GQDs nanosheets. Meanwhile, the TFN-50 OSN membrane maintains a high RDB rejection of 99.2%. Furthermore, the TFN-50 OSN membrane exhibits excellent durability performance in the harsh polar solvent DMF and NMP even at high temperature (80 °C) for more than 300 h, and still possesses an RDB rejection of 98.0%, which provides its vast potential for industrial application.
The detailed contributions of authors are as follows:
Shuxuan Li: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing - reviewing & editing.
Shaoxiao Liu: Methodology, Writing - manuscript preparation, reviewing & editing.
Baowei Su: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - reviewing & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration.
Xueli Gao: Supervision, Methodology.
Congjie Gao: Supervision, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22078310).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.memsci.2023.121960.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Robust polyamide-PTFE hollow fibre membranes for harsh organic solvent nanofiltration
Chem. Eng. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V.N. Francis, J.Y. Chong, G. Yang, L. Che, R. Wang, Robust polyamide-PTFE hollow fibre membranes for harsh organic solvent nanofiltration, Chemical Engineering Journal, 452 (2023) 139333.
